title: Mobile Subscription Refinement Model
subtitle: Target feed, subscription overlay, grounded event schema, and package-learning loop for Living Docs mobile.
scope: Subscription/refinement product model across tickets #27, #28, #31, #32, #33, and #35. Current runtime and transport implementation are tracked separately in `docs/mobile-companion-workstream.json`.
sourceCoverage: GitHub issues #27, #28, #31, #32, #33, and #35 inspected through GitHub tooling on 2026-04-17, plus `docs/mobile-mockups-subscription.html` from branch `mockup/subscription-ux` and the coupled implementation/runtime doc `docs/mobile-companion-workstream.json`.
objective: Keep the target subscription/refinement model coherent across package defaults, user-scoped refinements, grounded commit-time events, feed interaction mechanics, privacy boundaries, and the learning loop back into package revisions.
successCondition: A worker can understand the intended product behavior, data flow, coupled implementation seam, and unresolved architectural choices without re-deriving the vision sessions.

callouts.title: How to use this doc
items: Use this doc for the target subscription/refinement model across #27/#28/#31/#32/#33/#35.
Use `docs/mobile-companion-workstream.json` for current runtime, repo artifacts, transport delivery, and verification gates.
When implementation constraints conflict with target behavior, keep the model sharp here and record the compromise explicitly in the implementation doc instead of blending the two.

callout.title: Current model state
items: The mobile product is a refinement interface that happens to show events, not a feed reader with subscription settings attached.

The subscription surface sits as a sibling JSON overlay to living-doc structure: role packages generate defaults, user refinements stay user-scoped, and deleting the refinement layer falls back cleanly to role defaults.
Grounded claim generation at commit time is the semantic spine: session memory explains intent, the diff proves evidence, and only their grounded intersection becomes a claim.
The feed is prioritized, block-level, and evidence-first. Cards carry origin, transition, evidence, intent, disagreement affordances, and optional grounding warnings.
Aggregate mute/add/pin patterns should improve default packages over time, but only through reviewable proposals and aggregate statistics, never by silently mutating shared defaults.
The current transport implementation is already strong enough to carry this model, but it still emits doc-level messages rather than the block-level artifacts this document describes.

data.name: Role packages and user-scoped refinement overlay
notes: Subscription JSON structure must stay in sync with living doc structure, because blocks can be added, removed, renamed, split, or merged over time.

Mobile writes are scoped to the acting user's refinement surface only. A user can mutate their own `mutes[]`, `adds[]`, `last_active`, and `last_refined`, but cannot mutate package definitions, assignments, or another user's refinements.
Generated defaults are computed on the fly from the living doc and assigned role and are not persisted; only user-authored refinements persist.
Removed or renamed blocks should not silently erase historical refinements, because those refinements are signal for future package revision proposals.

data.name: Prioritized feed and inline refinement mechanics
notes: The feed is prioritized rather than chronological: regression at the top, then blocked, then digest cards, then progress.

Every card should carry origin strip, project/block/audience/timestamp header, transition tokens, diff-backed evidence, session-backed intent, grounding warnings when claim exceeds evidence, and one-tap actions such as See diff, Watch, Mute, Disagree, and Pin.
Refinement is cheap: swipe-left=mute, swipe-right=pin, with undo behavior and no settings detour required.

The unresolved interaction questions live here, not in the transport doc: digest density, undo behavior, inherited-vs-chosen visual grammar, passive signal versus explicit interaction, and disagreement mechanics.

data.name: Grounded claim event model
notes: At commit time, AI sees a unique window where session working memory, living-doc pre-state, and the diff align. After the commit, session memory decays fast.
The grounding rule is load-bearing: the diff is the arbiter of block status, the session is the arbiter of intent, and only the grounded claim writes to the living doc.
The target output is structured per block, with before/after, evidence, honest status, and open questions. That output should then feed both the mobile surface and a separate living-doc update pass.
This model is not asking for richer copy on doc-level messages; it is asking for a different artifact shape.

data.name: Contested-state dialogue and My map
notes: Block detail has two designed states: consensus and contested. A disagreement should turn a block into dialogue over an architectural claim, not just append a comment.
The user's own subscription surface should be visible through `My map`, with pinned, inherited, chosen, and muted counts grouped by project.
Passive signal counts as refinement signal, but it should not quietly become visible product behavior without an explicit architectural choice.
Watcher counts are aggregate-only and public; individual refinements stay private.

data.name: Aggregate refinement to package-learning loop
notes: Refinements should not die as personal preference noise. Aggregated mute/add/pin patterns should become proposals for improving default role packages.
The output should be a diff or PR against `subscriptions.json`, reviewed like any other code change.
The privacy boundary is aggregate only: team-level attention patterns are visible, but individual refinements are not exposed.
Minimum sample size, conflicting signals, and whether aggregate hints belong in the mobile UI remain explicit open questions.

data.name: Implementation-knowledge as a feed-worthy source
notes: The proposed `implementation-knowledge` convergence type carries prior-project intelligence with provenance: constraints, scripts, pitfalls, references, and recipes.
Its status progression is itself feed-worthy: `unacknowledged`, `acknowledged`, `incorporated`, or `dismissed` could all matter to the right subscriber.
This gives the subscription model a future class of events that are neither raw code updates nor generic notifications, but structured organizational memory becoming actionable.

data.name: Commit-time grounded event pipeline
notes: The current transport spine already exists: repo-local manifest, workflow trigger, feed branch, and mobile sync.
What is still missing is the semantic step between commit and feed item: a grounded event artifact per block, carrying intent, evidence, and honest status rather than doc-level prose.
This is the main coupling seam between the implementation workstream and this model doc.

data.name: Subscription storage and multi-writer boundary
notes: The model wants one multi-tenant file or surface that holds packages, assignments, and per-user refinements, but the storage location is still unresolved.
That choice determines conflict handling, write latency, and whether the source of truth can remain repo-local or needs a sidecar service.

data.name: Subscription routing location
notes: Routing can live client-side, server-side, or as a hybrid pre-computed per-user feed. Each option changes cost, privacy, offline-first behavior, and latency.
The model does not need this answered before it is legible, but implementation work should not quietly decide it by accident.

data.name: Block identity and rename semantics
notes: Rename semantics depend on block identity. A stable opaque ID makes rename a no-op; a path-like slug makes rename look like remove plus add.
This decision is load-bearing for both subscription durability and grounded event routing.

data.name: Privacy boundary and aggregate watcher model
notes: The intended privacy line is already clear: individual refinements stay private, aggregate stats can be public.
What remains open is where those aggregates are computed, how watcher counts are materialized, and whether any aggregate nudge belongs inside the mobile UI.

data.name: Subscription mockup and card grammar proof
data.currentCoverage: The mockup branch defines the feed, onboarding, block detail, and My map surfaces with the intended visual grammar and inline interactions.
data.nextStep: Turn the mockup into a canonical set of model states: card anatomy, digest behavior, disagreement state, and origin grammar.
gaps: Swipe vocabulary, undo behavior, digest density, and passive-signal effects remain open exploration areas.
The model still lacks a canonical card-level JSON shape that matches the mockup exactly.

data.name: Grounded event schema proof
data.currentCoverage: The grounding rule and a sample artifact shape exist in issue form, but not yet as a canonical schema consumed by the feed.
data.nextStep: Define one concrete JSON or YAML contract for grounded block events and test it against a real living doc touched by a commit.
gaps: Where the artifact lives is still undecided.
The deterministic handoff from structured event to living-doc update is still only a leaning, not a fixed rule.

data.name: Subscription overlay and user-write boundary proof
data.currentCoverage: The invariants are defined: defaults derive from role packages, refinements are additive, user writes are scoped, and orphaned refinements should not be silently discarded.
data.nextStep: Choose block identity and storage location, then write one reference `subscriptions.json` example that demonstrates rename, orphan, and user-write behavior.

data.name: Package-learning loop proof
data.currentCoverage: The model and privacy boundary are clear: aggregate only, role-grouped, and emitted as reviewable proposals rather than silent changes.
data.nextStep: Define minimum sample size, conflicting-signal handling, and one canonical proposal output shape.
gaps: No policy exists yet for whether aggregate hints appear in the mobile UI.

data.name: Implementation-knowledge convergence proof
data.currentCoverage: The proposed convergence type has item kinds, status progression, and provenance rules defined in issue form.
data.nextStep: Add the convergence type to `scripts/living-doc-registry.json` and render one sample section so its events can be reasoned about concretely.
gaps: The type is not yet in the registry.

data.name: Keep the subscription model separate from the implementation workstream
notes: This document carries the target model; `docs/mobile-companion-workstream.json` carries current code, runtime, transport, and verification truth.
The split is deliberate so the living doc stays a sharper reasoning surface instead of collapsing target model and implementation status into one mixed artifact.

data.name: Diff is arbiter of evidence; session is arbiter of intent
notes: Only the grounded intersection of session memory and diff evidence should become the block-level claim written into the living doc or surfaced in the feed.
Intent and evidence still travel with the event, but they are not interchangeable.

data.name: Choose block identity scheme for rename-safe subscriptions
notes: Opaque IDs make rename mostly a no-op; path-like slugs make rename look like delete plus add.
This choice affects subscriptions, event routing, and whether historical refinements survive ordinary document evolution.

data.name: Choose where grounded event artifacts live
notes: The current candidate locations are extended commit trailers, repo files under `.living-doc/commits/`, or an external store.
The tradeoff is between offline-first simplicity, push capability, privacy, and multi-repo aggregation.

data.name: Choose where subscription routing runs
notes: Routing can happen client-side, server-side, or as a hybrid pre-computed feed.
The right answer depends on team size, push requirements, infrastructure appetite, and how hard we want to preserve offline-first behavior.

data.name: Choose the refinement write-back path
notes: The viable options are direct git commits from mobile, a sidecar store, or a thin API layer that syncs back to `subscriptions.json`.
That choice determines merge-conflict behavior, latency, and whether `subscriptions.json` remains the practical source of truth.

data.name: Set thresholds and conflict rules for package-learning proposals
notes: The model needs an explicit minimum sample size and a policy for conflicting signals such as 50 percent muted and 50 percent pinned.
It also needs a decision on whether aggregate hints are visible only in review tooling or can subtly appear in the mobile product itself.
